topics.title: Former Colleague Relationships and Professional Fallout
topics.summary: The studio explores past professional relationships, discussing the complexities of personal feelings versus public personas when colleagues leave the show or face legal disputes.

topics.title: Artie Lange's Career Move and Show Dynamics
topics.summary: The studio discusses Artie Lange's transition to television, the production of a history of the show's signature segments, and various interactions with former cast members.

topics.title: Mel Gibson and Michael Richards Controversy
topics.summary: The studio discusses the fallout from Michael Richards' racial remarks on television, the marketing of Mel Gibson's film Apokolipto, and personal anecdotes regarding relationships and grooming.

topics.title: Celebrity Gossip and Production Anecdotes
topics.summary: The studio discusses celebrity rumors involving Pam Anderson, Britney Spears, and Michael Richards while taking a call from a listener regarding a scripted comedy.

topics.title: Michael Richards' On-Set Behavior
topics.summary: The studio recounts chaotic moments from a production involving Michael Richards, including his erratic behavior on set, his refusal to use safety equipment during stunts, and his reputation for being difficult to work with.

topics.title: Tracy Morgan Arrested for DWI
topics.summary: The show discusses the arrest of comedian Tracy Morgan for driving while intoxicated and shares anecdotes regarding a colleague named Ralph.

topics.title: Porn Star DeBella's Career and Family Conflict
topics.summary: The studio features an interview with porn star DeBella, who discusses her transition into the industry at age 50, her open marriage, and the resulting estrangement from her daughter.

topics.title: DeBella's Adult Film Career and Performance
topics.summary: The show features an interview with adult film star DeBella regarding her career, personal life, and a demonstration involving a mechanical device.

topics.title: Beetlejuice Merchandise and Studio Cleanup
topics.summary: The studio discusses the release of a new Beetlejuice film, promotes related merchandise, addresses concerns regarding workplace cleanliness in the kitchen area, and clarifies the logistics of holiday gift-giving for the staff.

topics.title: Holiday Gift Logistics and Parental Guidance
topics.summary: The studio discusses the logistical challenges of providing holiday gifts to a large staff while addressing a caller's concerns regarding their son's access to pornography.

topics.title: Crazy Cabbie Benefit and Comedy Insights
topics.summary: The studio features a discussion regarding a benefit concert for "Crazy Cabbie" following his release from prison, followed by a segment where Greg Fitzsimmons shares an anecdote about Michael Richards' behavior during a comedy set.

topics.title: Standup Comedy and Promotional Announcements
topics.summary: The studio discusses the nuances of stand-up comedy, promotes upcoming live performances and a comic book benefit, and features a segment regarding heavy metal vocal techniques before concluding with a discussion on internal show promotions.

topics.title: Riley Martin and Comedy News Updates
topics.summary: The studio features a heated exchange with Riley Martin regarding his business claims followed by news segments on comedy club policies and local media stories.

topics.title: Radio Industry Trends and Celebrity News
topics.summary: The studio discusses the decline of terrestrial radio, a variety of celebrity anecdotes including an incident involving a lizard in Brooklyn, and various news items regarding pop culture and local events.

topics.title: Cultural Language, Casino Policies, and Crime Reports
topics.summary: The studio explores various topics including the reclamation of derogatory terms, potential smoking bans in Atlantic City casinos, celebrity culture, reports on Fidel Castro's health, a fatal shooting in Queens, and personal anecdotes regarding childhood mishaps.

topics.title: Iraq War Strategy and Global News
topics.summary: The show covers a variety of topics including personal anecdotes about family discipline, reports on tragic incidents involving children, the escalating insurgency in Iraq, Pope Benedict XVI's visit to Turkey, and upcoming entertainment events.

transcript.text: Hey now, good morning everybody. Welcome to Wednesday.
Hey now, hey now, hey now, hey now, hey now, hey now, hey now, hey now, hey now, hey now.
Oh boy, oh boy, oh boy. Well...
Hey now.
Heard from Paul Mooney, he's dropping the n-word from his act, so if we can have him in, that's a very big story.
Yeah, I'd like to hear him tell a few jokes about it.
Make a plea.
I've had a press release, I am not dropping him from my act.
Media alert, Paul Mooney performs the first time after abolishing his use of the n-word.
That was a big deal.
See an historical event, Paul Mooney and Dick Gregory Live, with no n-word from Paul Mooney.
Now, um, Arty, since people are calling for a ban on the word, you feel no pressure?
I don't think anyone's going to pay much attention to what I do here, so I'm going to skate right under the radar.
And nobody's waiting for Arty's decision.
What's up, my nigga?
You don't think it'll even make a bluff?
By the way, Dick...
I'm adding a couple of n-words, actually.
Dick Gregory's book is called Nigger. Actually, I read it.
What's he going to do about that?
That was a hundred years ago. What's he going to do? Change the name? African Upper America?
Well, he hasn't said he's going to stop using the n-word.
What about Richard Pryor's album?
Only Paul Mooney.
Good morning, everybody, and welcome to the show.
You know we do it here every day, and we try to entertain you in some way here on Sirius Satellite Radio.
The future of radio?
I was watching the American Music Awards, I saw a few ads for us and a few ads for XM.
Oh, did you?
Yeah. And I read an article again yesterday about how the satellite companies may merge, and I wonder about that.
Did they have any...
New information?
Or information about contact between the two companies.
They claimed that it was a very small discussion about it.
Oh, really?
But I don't know.
It was probably you and me.
Yeah, it was some blog from, like, Brooklyn or something.
Well, yeah, some guy who's not interested or has no interest in either company.
It's something we never read except we're involved, so people send me all these articles.
Yeah.
And who knows what will happen in the future, but satellite radio is happening.
I mean, millions of people keep joining.
You heard what Bubba said, 6,000 or something a day, or what was it?
6,000 a day, that he said.
Yeah, it's pretty wild.
I don't know if he's been doing the count, but that's what he said.
Of course, Bubba was the same guy who was shooting in a diaper yesterday.
Right, so I don't know if you want to...
How'd that go?
I know they had some sort of shit off where...
They were weighing it, because it had to have some volume.
Well, they were seeing how long it would take for a guy to shit whoever could hold it in the longest and take enemas,
and then they weighed their bowel movements.
Everybody went?
I believe so.
I didn't hear it.
Ronnie heard it, said it was real funny, and I heard them talking about it.
They took enemas, too?
Yeah, there was some sort of liquid and pills they took to really make them shit their brains out.
Because enemas aren't going to go in your diaper.
Now, I think it was a pill that Bubba's mom knows about.
I mean, I catch the show here and there.
I missed this whole week.
I don't know what they've been doing.
Maybe it's better.
Yeah, it sounds like it was a shitty week.
I can't even finish...
I can't even finish my coffee by the conversation.
Yeah, it was pretty wild.
Ronnie said it was real funny.
I'm sure.
I was waiting to hear it, and then I had to get out of the car.
Can you imagine the visual of all those guys in the studio in diapers?
Were they just in their diapers? No pants?
I don't know.
I'm trying not to visualize it.
Ronnie said one guy threw up from the stink in there.
Like, actually vomited.
Why would you even want to...
You know, that sounds like...
That's Richard Christie territory.
Gary handed me a business card.
I'm obsessed with it of a guy who I started out with in radio,
and he ran into him in the Hamptons or somewhere,
and the guy now sells Hamptons real estate
and does some internet radio thing he claims.
But it was weird, because here was a guy I started out with,
and then he became a big program director over at, like, NEW,
and now he's like...
He's out of the business?
Yeah, it's always weird to me.
Like, I always wonder what happens to guys I started with.
Are you talking about Ted Harvard?
No, Ted Utz.
Ted Utz.
Wrong Ted.
Ted Harvard's over at E.
The television guy from your college thing.
Yeah, no, this is a guy...
This is Ted Utz, who used to run NEW.
Yeah, he ran...
He was a disc jockey, weekend disc jockey,
WR&W, the first radio station I worked for.
I totally know that name from just reading about radio and stuff,
so he must have been kind of a big deal, right?
Licensed real estate salesperson.
Well, I remember the big mistake he made.
What was his mistake?
He bad mouthed NEW, I guess, once he left it.
He was working for another company.
What'd he do?
I don't know anything about this.
Oh, God, we tormented him.
The big mistake he made was you came home one day
and there was a message on your answering machine that was threatening,
and it took you three days to figure out who it was, and it was Ted.
Oh.
This is when he worked at NEW and we were at K-Rock, and he's like,
"I know where you live," but he thought it was a joke,
but you didn't know who it was for three days,
and when you figured it out with him, you were really upset.
Oh, yeah, I don't remember that.
Well, I'm talking about a career mistake.
He bad mouthed NEW after he left,
and we were at the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame Awards one year,
me, Mel, Terry, whatever,
and Ted came over to say hello to everybody at the table.
Mel shunned him.
Oh, yeah, Mel takes that shit seriously.
He shunned him, and the guy's hand was hanging in the air,
and Mel refused to take it, refused to talk to him, refused to look at him.
You talk about vendettas.
I mean, Mel, when we worked at NBC,
we had a traffic copter girl named Donna Fidusha,
who now is a Fox newscaster, Fox television newscaster.
I see her on weekends and late nights sometimes.
Donna Fidusha? Yeah, Donna Fidusha.
I remember, well, that was her full name.
According to you immature guys.
Well, if you'd been working with us back then,
you would have been right in on that.
Are you kidding me? Oh, my God.
You know, we had her crying a few times.
She would have cried every day if you were there.
I thought it was funny back then. I think it's funnier now.
She was actually a really nice girl, and we liked her,
and when we got fired, we were going to come over to K-Rock,
which was one of Mel's stations.
And he said to me, "I would never hire her."
I said, "Why is that?"
He said, "She was on the radio with you guys one time,
and you were making some kind of comments about me."
And she said, "What did she say?"
I remember this exactly because it's so little what she did.
You were talking about how Mel had just bought WKTU.
We were at NBC. For $90 million.
And you joked around, and you're like,
"Yeah, Mel, some crazy Jewish guy lives in Miami.
I don't know what it's like laying on the beach listening to a station."
And he said, "I heard he's really tough on people," something like that.
And Donna said something like, "Oh, yeah, I heard that too."
You said 50 things, and she said--
And she said, "I heard that too."
And I think she was just trying to be on the air.
I don't think she heard it.
That was it. Mel said he would never hire us.
But what about all the shitty things I said?
He said, "Well, I got to put up with you."
Well, even in the Ted Utz thing, I said, "Mel, what did he say?"
He said, "I don't care."
You're talking about my station. You're talking about me.
Yeah, yeah, yeah. He's all serious about this radio business.
I think he's lightened up a little bit, but not much.
But I must have stripped assurance from Corleone.
Can I tell you one great story?
As time goes by and his position becomes stronger,
how do I know he won't attempt any individual vendetta?
I heard vendetta. I had to get that out. I'm sorry.
You know our friend that we worked with at Philly, Andy Bloom.
He told me the story, and I asked Mel.
Mel said it never happened. Remember he left the company?
Right.
And when you leave the company, Mel gets very upset.
So Andy said it was like a year later, he went to an event,
and he went to talk to Mel, and Mel just wouldn't turn around and talk to him.
He said, "Come on, Mel. We know each other. Mel wouldn't turn around."
And then he said, "Mel turned to the person he was with."
And he goes, "I feel wind blowing at the back of my head,
but I don't know what it is."
And it was Andy talking to him.
He will embarrass you publicly. He's good at that.
You know, I want to say Mel's over the line,
but I do have a problem with people who leave.
You know, I've even noticed that in my career trajectory
that, you know, when someone leaves the show...
Boy, your voice is deep today.
Even though someone has done nothing wrong, I do get crazy.
You know, I don't like to be left, which is very childish.
It's very odd because it's not like you're a lover or something.
No, but it's like a loyalty thing.
But maybe they're still loyal, even though they had to move on.
Yeah, there's no rationale.
I'm not saying that. I'm not proud of it.
I'm just telling you the way it is. I'm a very flawed human being.
Oh, well, stop with the flawed.
Sounds like you're human.
That's a part of being human.
You know, Stuttering John used to call work in here...
He used to call it the firm.
He used to say, like in that book, "The Firm,"
"If you leave, they'll chase you down and they'll kill you."
Right.
Well, that's the way it is.
You know, it's just every sort of relationship I had with people who leave...
Although I shouldn't say that.
Like, there are people who have left that I'm still very friendly with and feel good about.
Who?
Robin Rodzinski left. I love her.
Oh, okay. That's a good one.
Mark Cronin left. I feel great about him.
In fact, he did me a favor.
I'm just joking.
Also...
No, but there is a certain type of person that leaves. I can't explain.
Well, they do it properly.
You know, they're not assholes.
Right.
I remember when I left the port, man, that those were jobs that weren't easy to come by.
And it was considered like a great favor that I had this gig.
I'm telling you, when I went and talked to the guy who had gotten me the job,
a real nice guy, but, you know, a little intimidating without even knowing he's intimidating.
You know, so I said, I was 23 and I was like, "He called me in."
So I'm hearing stuff, some of the gang is talking that you're not going to be here for a long time.
I said, "Oh, no, actually, I was going to tell you around Christmas I'm thinking of leaving."
He goes, "What are you going to do?"
What are you going to do?
I said, "I don't know. I want to try to make it in show business."
And he was like, "What?"
I went to be like a comedian or something.
And I said, "So I'm thinking of leaving."
And then there was like a long pause and I went, "That's all right."
Well, those guys, you know what it is, though?
No one wants anyone to leave and do really well either.
That's another thing.
It's kind of like they probably really liked you.
You know, it's threatening your way.
The explanation was...
Yeah, a job like that.
He didn't think I was going to do anything.
He was saying that he was very calmly without trying to be insulting, saying,
"Look, you can go to college. It's a $60,000 job you got here."
He goes, "I got people giving their right arm for it.
You're close with someone he was close to, so I did you a favor."
And I'm like, "I know. I don't mean to be disrespectful in any way. I just, you know..."
And I got to go.
And yeah, it was weird.
Just like, "Hey, I'm trying to do you a favor.
What the hell do you think you are?"
Right.
And like, Jackie left the show and oddly enough, I have no problem with Jackie leaving the show.
I really don't.
Well, actually, he did do you a favor.
But you did at the time, at the moment.
No, but I never disliked Jackie.
No, but you felt it was done improperly.
I did.
And you felt a little burnt, I thought.
No.
No, I didn't.
I don't think that happened at all.
I think that you were quite frankly ready for that to happen.
Yeah.
I mean, it had been a difficult association all those years, and you were saying...
You said, "I'm tired of fighting."
Yeah, but I didn't feel badly...
To keep it.
I didn't feel bad about Jackie.
I understood he left.
He felt he should have certain things the way he wanted him, and I understand that.
I've left jobs because of that.
I really did, but...
But can I ask you about that?
Because it was a long period of time where you didn't feel comfortable socializing with him.
No.
In fact, we used to write each other all the time, but I know that I was like...
It was weird because Jackie was still holding on to the fact that somehow I never returned
his call or something like that, but I think it was more coming from Jackie than me.
Jackie was the one that we invited him on the show.
He was the one who had the...
He didn't want to come on the show.
He couldn't be a guest.
He had a lot of things he couldn't do.
Right, but I didn't feel that way.
I mean, I didn't like the way he was doing business and stuff, but personally, I always liked Jackie.
And I don't know, things got weird between me and Stuttering John, but I didn't really have anything against Stuttering John.
I thought that some of the stuff Stuttering John did when he was here was a little sneaky, but I still liked the guy.
But when he left, did you feel...
I don't know, it was just weird.
I think that's funny, because I was thinking about this earlier today, and I was like,
"Ooh, if you were going to ask ten provocative questions of Howard, one of them would have been about Stuttering John
and that whole relationship and what goes on now and why is it the way it is or whatever."
Well, things are weird.
You know, like Scott Einsinger has written to me two times.
Oh, yeah.
He was our television producer for a long time, and he was like one of my best friends personally.
I think he was your best friend.
Yeah, he really was.
For a while.
Yeah.
I was going to ask you about that one.
And we have, you know, since the law, you know, I had to sue over that show that he was involved in.
Right.
And I felt really hurt that he didn't come to my defense, even though the whole thing was settled out.
So I heard from him recently, and he said, "You know, this is killing me."
He goes, "You know, I feel terrible."
And he did apologize.
He said, "I think I did make a mistake, and I really would like to, you know, see if we can repair our relationship.
You know, I've got a kid now, and I'd like you to meet the kid."
Well, that's no reason to ask him that.
No, nobody else.
There was a list of things and reasons why.
I thought he teased him like he don't want to meet the kid.
And quite frankly, you know, you just miss the relationship, as I do.
And I wrote him back, and I said, "You know, I just kind of don't understand what the hell happened there,
and I need an explanation."
And he explained it all to me, and it was perfectly reasonable, you know, what he said.
But I said to him, "I hear what you're saying.
I just don't know how to get this relationship back on track, to be honest with you."
Well, people sometimes, you know, because I remember the, you know,
we had our falling out with Dee Snider, let's say.
It's sort of like more on that level.
And, you know, when Dee came in and, you know, was like, "I want to, I miss our relationship with you,"
because he had a personal relationship with you.
"I miss my relationship with you. My wife misses you.
We, you know, we had this thing, and we can't."
And, you know, sometimes you can't get that back if it's broken.
Yeah, it's hard.
You might forgive each other, but you can't go back.
That's how we sort of left it in the email.
I just kind of said, you know, no hard feelings.
And what you said is absolutely reasonable, and I just--
You don't understand it, and whatever.
I'm not that great at repairing stuff like this.
I don't know. I would feel awkward and still do not know how to get past the feelings of I was somehow betrayed.
But you're also not in close proximity.
You know, those things take work.
They do. They do.
And you don't have the time or energy for it.
Here's something I've always wanted to ask you about that particular relationship.
Before the lawsuit thing, before that, he worked here for a long time.
You were absolutely great friends, and you absolutely loved him.
When he left to go work for another company, I really sensed the feeling of weirdness.
It wasn't just like, hey, good luck. But I think it was.
No, I only wished him good luck.
I don't know. He used to goof on the fact that he laughed.
Oh, I thought it was funny that he was going to Big Brother. It was a show I didn't understand.
No, it wasn't Big Brother. It was the Amazing Race.
Amazing Race.
Which is still on.
And he said it was going to be a hit.
Yeah, and I was just like, wow.
But no, I was never angry. I understood it.
No, you weren't angry.
I was sad to see him go. I really like working with him.
But yeah, I remember this vividly, and I wouldn't say it was anger.
Right.
And I wouldn't-- I don't even know whether to call it a "bibble."
But there was a certain-- like, it wasn't a happy send-up.
I'm not talking about the day he left. I'm talking about those months after.
You just-- it always seemed to be like you'd always crack a joke.
It was-- do you understand what I'm saying? Like, it wasn't--
Yeah, but I honestly am telling you, I understood why he was leaving.
And it didn't bother you at all that he left. Not at all.
Oh, of course it did. I was losing a friend.
I mean, he was going off to California. I was really sad to see him go.
I remember what Gary's talking about, because I remember it clearly,
because I didn't know Scott enough to say anything.
So during those discussions, I had to be quiet.
So it was a week of, like, intense goofing on him.
Yeah, but I thought it was funny.
But I'm sitting here, he's sitting there saying I was, you know,
I was happy for him or whatever.
I remember those conversations being as uncomfortable as your conversations
with Ross about his marriage.
Absolutely.
I-- listen, when I'm on the air, I go from maximum to maximum.
It is what makes me so unique.
You know what I'm saying?
You know what I'm saying?
Is what comes out on the air part of who you are?
You know, in other words, you can say I'm joking.
Of course.
But was it a feeling you were having?
Absolutely. I was having a hard time with him leaving.
Yes.
But I wasn't mad at him.
And that was the truth, that his-- but it came out in a very angry way.
Yes, it did.
And a lot of times, anger covers up hurt feelings.
Right. Absolutely. Yes, accurate.
And that's the expression. And you were really angry.
I was bummed to see the guy go. I really was.
But, you know, on a personal level, and, you know, it just-- it was bummer.
But, like, he thought I was angry with him, like, mad at him for leaving,
and I wasn't.
But it came across that way.
No. And, in fact, after he left, like, he went off to California,
and he-- and one of the things he said to me is, like, you know,
"Gee, I had heard through the grave of Howard never gets in touch with me."
But I was like, "Dude, you went to California.
I got hundreds of people that work with us.
I barely have time to sit and connect with them."
The reason we used to connect was he--
He was here.
He was here, and we all hung out together.
And then when he left, it was like, well, you know,
I wasn't, like, not going to talk to him anymore.
But it was like he was in California with a new job,
and I-- you know, how often do I get to call you,
and you're way more close to me than Scott was?
He'd never call me.
Exactly.
That's what I'm saying.
So I don't call anybody.
So he was like, "Gee, nobody calls me."
I never used to call him before.
I used to say, "Hey, you want to come over?
That'd be one thing."
But I don't sit and call people.
So what happened with, say, the stuttering--
The stuttering gentleman.
The stuttering gentleman's like, "I left out.
I haven't heard from Howard."
I'm like, "When did you hear from me when you were here?"
"I used to come to your house."
"Yeah, you lived in New York.
You were around.
You're a good guy to hang out with.
You're not here anymore."
I don't know.
He would claim, if you were here right now,
that several times on his return to New York,
he tried to make dinner plans with you,
and was going to do that.
Let me tell you something.
Walt Sabo and Jeremy Coleman called me last night.
How do we get that?
We want to have lunch with you.
I said, "I don't do lunch."
They go, "Let's have dinner."
Just as friends, because we enjoy each other's company.
I said, "I'm going to make some time for dinner."
Now, I go, "I don't want to go to dinner with anyone,
and I love those guys."
Same with stuttering John.
I mean, make time for dinner.
I don't go socially to dinner at all during the week, ever.
No, this is weekends.
When you'd be in town for weekends,
it would be like a Saturday night.
You want to know something?
We invited him over to come to our house,
and he told us he was too busy.
So I don't know what the fuck he's talking about.
Sometimes I'm busy.
He takes it that if I'm busy, he doesn't like me.
I got fucking plans, or not.
You know, but I don't know.
It's just weird.
Yeah, I mean, I often think that--
Have you talked to him aside from Ross's wedding?
I talked to him at Ross's wedding,
and I'd say Beth and Susanna talk at least two or three times a week.
Right.
They have maintained a relationship.
Yeah.
Would you like to know an uncomfortable moment
that you're probably not even aware of?
If you remember, I think we were at Ross's rehearsal dinner.
It was coming up on John's 40th birthday,
and John's wife was making sort of a tribute tape.
And I think what I heard through the grapevine
is that you were somehow approached
to do something for the tape, and you didn't want to.
No, I wasn't.
I was never approached to do anything for the tape,
but I wouldn't have done it.
I wouldn't have done it if he was working for me.
I don't want to be on a tribute tape.
I don't make tributes.
Here's the tribute.
Leave me alone.
I did it, but for the record, those things are awkward.
Yeah, no, I did it too.
All of a sudden, like 3,000 miles away,
someone's watching you on a tape and a closet and a wedding?
And you don't know what's going on.
Yeah, right at a party where everybody's quiet.
Those things never work.
Let me be, will you?
So John can feel good about himself.
Oh, I mean, it was a surprise for him.
But, you know, those things don't work, the rollout tapes.
In a way, John gives himself too much credit.
Like, I'm so busted up over his leaving, I can't see him.
It's not that.
Oh, I don't think he's thinking you're so busted up
over his leaving.
I think he thinks he was just exiled like he means nothing.
That's right.
That's how he feels.
No, I invited him to my home, and he turned me down.
So there's the bottom line.
I have nothing to say.
Go argue with that.
I wouldn't know anyway, because he doesn't talk to me.
Or don't.
You guys have had no contact?
No, no, we talked at Ross's wedding,
and we sort of tried to get together.
Why is it weird?
You explained it then.
It's just kind of like the procedure.
Well, we had a little bit of a-- you know,
he got weird with me at the end.
Yeah.
I've got to tell you, he was a bag of emotions.
He blew up on the air like it was three days before he left.
He yelled at Rob and everything.
And then he started a fight with me.
And I go, John, we were the whole way.
I go, John, what's going on?
And he just walked up, and he hugged me?
You know, like he was all over the place that last week.
It was very difficult for him.
